fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that in-memory filesystem thing we talked about in the last planning session. Basically developers are super frustrated with tests that write to real disk — things are slow, there's leftover temp garbage everywhere, and some of the weirder edge cases (you know, the loop stuff, the empty-vs-not-empty situations) are basically impossible to reproduce reliably on CI. We want a clean engine that lives entirely in memory so tests are fast and deterministic.

The engine needs to handle all the usual suspects: directories (create, list, delete, move, navigate), files (read/write in different modes, copy, rename, measure size), symbolic links, and seekable file handles. It should behave like a real POSIX filesystem would, including the error categories — we want those normalized the same way we did for the error handling in that storage abstraction layer from the config module.

Important: this can't be one giant file. Split it up properly, separate the I/O adapter from the actual logic. The adapter reads a JSON program from stdin and writes results line by line to stdout. Refer to how we structured the path resolution piece in the existing navigation utilities — same separation idea applies here.

There are a few tricky bits around temp directory naming, how trailing newlines behave on line reads, and what happens when you follow a link that loops back on itself. Make sure those are handled correctly.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in. For temp dirs, they should live under `/.tmp_rand0` at the root, and the actual name is just the provided prefix plus a counter suffix like `rand0`, `rand1`, etc. So if somebody calls create_temp with prefix `foo` twice, that should produce `/.tmp_rand0/foorand0` and then `/.tmp_rand0/foorand1`. That counter is per-prefix and keeps incrementing on each successive call with the same prefix.

Also on line behavior, we want line splitting to be trailing-newline agnostic. A file containing `a\nb\nc\n` should give exactly 3 lines, so the final newline does not create an extra empty trailing line. And a zero-byte file should yield `line_count=0` with no line entries. For `raf_read`, the output is always two lines: `read_length=<n>` and then `read_text=<json-quoted-content>`. `read_length=<n>` is the number of bytes actually read, so it can be smaller than what was requested if there are fewer bytes left. If the cursor is already at EOF, emit `read_length=0` and `read_text=""`.

One more small path detail: the parent of `/` is `/`, so the `parent` operation on `/` emits exactly `parent=/`. That’s just path math and does not depend on whether anything exists there. And for symlink cycles, if following links loops back on itself, like `/foo -> /bar` and `/bar -> /foo`, then `type` with `follow_links=true` should return `type=not_found` instead of erroring or spinning.

On the error side, please keep everything normalized as `error=<category>` and only use these exact category strings: `no_such_file_or_directory`, `is_a_directory`, `not_a_directory`, `file_exists`, `directory_not_empty`, `unknown_operation`. Same deal as the error normalization contract from Feature 7, with all failures surfacing as `error=<category>` using those stable strings exactly, as specified in `feature7_error_normalization.json`.

And just reaffirming the code organization piece since that came up too: we do want a dedicated path-resolution component, separate from the public operations layer and the execution adapter, and that piece should own POSIX path traversal, symlink following, and normalization, in line with the Scale-Driven Code Organization and SRP guidance in `start.md`.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main() async { final input = await stdin.transform(utf8.decoder).join(); final program = jsonDecode(input); final fs = InMemoryFileSystem(); final adapter = ExecutionAdapter(fs); final output = adapter.run(program); stdout.write(output); }

trigger_keywords: create_directory on existing directory
idempotent directory creation
mkdir no-op
creating a directory that already exists
recursive create already existing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Creating a directory that already exists as a directory is a successful no-op. The operation emits `created_directory=<path>` as if newly created. However, if a file already exists at that path, it fails with `error=not_a_directory`.

trigger_keywords: write mode default behavior
does write overwrite or append by default
default write mode
overwrite vs append
mode field on write operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The write operation defaults to overwrite mode, replacing all existing content. To preserve existing content and add at the end, pass `mode: 'append'`. Appending to a non-existent file creates it. For file handles (raf_write), the open mode determines the behavior: 'write' starts fresh, 'append' preserves existing content.

trigger_keywords: create_link output lines
what does create_link emit
symbolic link creation output
link_target line after create
how many lines does create_link produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The create_link operation emits two lines on success: `created_link=<path>` followed immediately by `link_target=<target>`. Both lines are always emitted together. The target is stored as the literal string passed regardless of whether it resolves to a real entity.

trigger_keywords: file handle open output
what does open emit
raf open result line
opening a file handle stdout
open operation output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The open operation emits `opened=<path>` (the resolved path of the file). The handle name is used in subsequent raf_* operations but is not echoed in the open output line itself.

trigger_keywords: exists without as parameter
exists default kind
exists op no as field
checking existence without specifying type
what does exists check by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `exists` operation accepts an optional `as` field specifying the kind to check against (file, directory, or link). When `as` is omitted, it checks for the existence of any entity at that path regardless of kind — a file, directory, or link all satisfy the check. The output is always `exists=true` or `exists=false`.

trigger_keywords: codebase module structure
how should the code be organized
file layout separation of concerns
god file prohibition
adapter vs core engine split
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be split into at minimum four distinct units: (1) a core model of the file tree and entity types, (2) a path-resolution component that handles traversal and link-following, (3) the public file-system operations layer, and (4) a separate execution adapter responsible only for reading JSON from stdin, routing to the core engine, and writing key=value lines to stdout. The core engine must have zero dependency on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Symbolic link cycle detection during link resolution: when following links results in a circular reference, the engine must return `type=not_found` rather than infinite-looping or throwing — tested in feature3_2_link_type.json case 3

The directory_not_empty error category triggered when attempting a non-recursive delete on a directory that contains children — tested in feature1_3_directory_delete.json and feature7_error_normalization.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify offhand — you'd need to dig into how the existing navigation and error-handling patterns were set up in the relevant modules, or check the test cases for the specific behavior you're unsure about.

feature1_1_directory_create.json.description: Create directories. A directory can be created at a path whose parent already exists; creating it recursively also materializes any missing ancestor directories. Creating a directory that already exists as a directory is a no-op success. The created directory's path is echoed back.

feature1_2_directory_list.json.description: List the immediate children of a directory, or recursively list all descendants. Each entry is reported as its kind (file/directory/link) and its path. Entries are emitted in a stable, lexicographically sorted order and the total count is reported first.

cases.expected_output:
created_directory=/d
created_file=/d/b
created_file=/d/a
created_directory=/d/sub
entry_count=3
entry=directory:/d/sub
entry=file:/d/a
entry=file:/d/b

created_directory=/a/b/c
created_file=/a/b/c/f.txt
entry_count=3
entry=directory:/a/b
entry=directory:/a/b/c
entry=file:/a/b/c/f.txt

feature1_3_directory_delete.json.description: Delete a directory. A non-recursive delete succeeds only when the directory is empty; deleting a non-empty directory non-recursively fails with a directory-not-empty category. A recursive delete removes the directory and all of its contents. The deleted path is echoed back.

feature1_4_directory_rename.json.description: Rename (move) a directory to a new path. Renaming onto a path whose parent does not exist fails, and renaming a source that does not exist fails, both with a not-found category. A successful rename reports the new path.

feature1_5_directory_navigation.json.description: Compute structural path properties of a directory without touching storage: the parent directory of a path, and the absolute form of a relative path resolved against the current working directory. The parent of the root directory is the root itself.

feature1_6_temp_directory.json.description: Create a uniquely-named temporary directory under a system temp area. Successive temp directories created with the same prefix receive distinct names by appending an incrementing suffix.

feature2_1_file_text.json.description: Write a string to a file and read it back as text. Writing reports the number of bytes (UTF-8 encoded) written; reading returns the JSON-quoted text content. Reading a zero-byte file yields an empty string.

feature2_2_file_bytes.json.description: Write a list of raw byte values to a file and read them back as bytes. The byte count is reported on write; reading returns the comma-joined byte values. A zero-byte file reads back as an empty byte sequence.

feature2_3_file_lines.json.description: Read a file as a list of text lines. The line count is reported first, followed by each line. Line splitting is trailing-newline agnostic: a final newline does not produce an extra empty line. A zero-byte file yields zero lines.

feature2_4_file_append.json.description: Append mode preserves existing file content and adds new content at the end, in contrast to the default write mode which overwrites. Appending to a non-existent file creates it.

feature2_5_file_length.json.description: Report the byte length of a file. A newly created file has length zero; after writing N bytes the length is N. Querying the length of a path that does not exist fails with a not-found category.

feature2_6_file_copy.json.description: Copy a file's content to a destination path, returning the destination path. If the destination already exists as a file, its content is overwritten by the source's content. Copying a non-existent source fails with a not-found category.

feature2_7_file_rename.json.description: Rename (move) a file to a new path, returning the new path. The content moves with the file and the old path no longer exists. Renaming a source that does not exist fails with a not-found category.

feature3_1_link_create_target.json.description: Create a symbolic link pointing at a target path, and read back the stored target. The link records the literal target string regardless of whether the target exists. Creating a link at a path already occupied by a file fails with an already-exists category.

feature3_2_link_type.json.description: Determine the kind of entity at a path with link following controlled by a flag. With link-following enabled, a link to a file reports as a file; with following disabled, it reports as a link. A link whose target chain forms a loop reports as not-found when followed.

feature3_3_link_resolve.json.description: Resolve all symbolic links in a path to its canonical real path. A link to a directory resolves to the directory's path, including when the link is nested within a traversal. Resolving an empty path fails with a not-found category.

feature3_4_link_delete.json.description: Delete a symbolic link itself without affecting the entity it points to. After unlinking, the link no longer exists but its former target remains.

cases.expected_output:
created_file=/foo
created_link=/bar
link_target=/foo
deleted=/bar
exists=true
exists=false

feature4_1_cwd.json.description: Get and set the current working directory. Setting the working directory to an existing directory updates it and echoes the new path; relative paths passed afterward resolve against it. Setting it to a path that does not exist fails with a not-found category, and setting it to a file fails with a not-a-directory category.

feature5_1_raf_sequential.json.description: Open a file handle and write bytes/text sequentially, then read them back. Writing reports the number of bytes written; the handle's length reflects accumulated writes. A single byte value can also be written and read back as raw bytes.

cases.expected_output:
created_file=/f
opened=/f
wrote_bytes=5
length=5
closed=h
text="hello"

feature5_2_raf_position.json.description: A file handle tracks a read/write cursor position. Reading advances the position; the cursor can be repositioned to an absolute offset and subsequent reads start from there. Reading more bytes than remain returns only the available bytes.

cases.expected_output:
wrote_bytes=5
opened=/f
read_byte=104
position=1
position=0
read_length=5
read_text="hello"

feature5_3_raf_truncate.json.description: Truncate an open file handle to a given length, discarding any content beyond that length. The file content reflects the truncation after the handle is closed.

feature6_1_exists.json.description: Test whether an entity exists at a path, interpreted as a specific kind (file/directory/link). A link is considered to exist as a link even when its target is missing, but does not exist as a file in that case.

feature6_2_entity_type.json.description: Report the entity kind at a path. The non-following type query reports file/directory/link/not-found at the tail of the path. The stat-based type follows links and reports the kind of the final target (e.g. a link to a file reports as a file), or not-found when nothing is there. The root directory and ancestors of root report as directories.

feature6_3_identical.json.description: Test whether two paths refer to the same underlying file system object. The same path compared with itself is identical; distinct files are not. Comparing paths where an entity does not exist fails with a not-found category.

feature7_error_normalization.json.description: All failures are surfaced as language-neutral POSIX-style error categories on an `error=` line, never as host-language exception artifacts. Categories include not-found (missing path), is-a-directory (file operation targeting a directory), not-a-directory (directory operation targeting a file), file-exists (creating over an existing entity), and directory-not-empty (non-recursive delete of a populated directory).
